s1_b1: Two toy carts collided on the table. One ended up dented. Noura's little brother says his cart was barely moving.
s1_b2: She wants one answer. Who was actually going faster, the moment they hit?
s1_b3: She looks at the dent. The bigger dent must mean the harder hit. Right?
s1_b4: So she guesses from the wreck. She's sure the dented one took the bigger hit, so it was the slower one.
s1_b5: Her brother runs it again. Same carts. This time the dented one was clearly the fast one. Her rule just broke.
s1_b6: She tries reading speed from how far they slid after. But a heavy cart and a light cart slide differently from the same hit.
s1_b7: Every clue lies on its own. The dent, the slide, the noise — none of them, alone, gives her the speed.
s1_b8: She wasn't short of clues. She was short of a conserved quantity — something the crash can't change.
s1_b9: Mass times velocity, added up for both carts. Before the hit and after — that total stays the same. Always.
s1_b10: And the energy of motion? That can drop — into the dent, the sound, the heat. How much it drops tells her what kind of collision this was.
s1_b11: Two rules. One that never changes. One that's allowed to. Together they pin down what really happened.
s1_b12: Before she argues another minute — she rebuilds the crash where she can measure it.
s1_b13: Collision Lab. She sets the two masses. Turns on the momentum arrows and the kinetic-energy number.
s1_b14: Here is the real job. Not pressing play. Predicting — before she does.
s1_b15: Given what each cart does after — work backward. What speed must each have had before, so the total momentum balances?
s1_b16: Then she presses play. And she watches the arrows and the energy number — not the carts.
s1_b17: After — she explains the gap. If the energy barely dropped, they bounced. If it collapsed, they crushed together.
s1_b18: She adjusts a mass. Predicts again. Three runs. That's all she gets.
s1_b19: Her first prediction is wrong. That is not the problem. That is the point. Each miss tells her which clue had fooled her.
s1_b20: Now she answers her brother. Not from the dent — from the numbers. And she can show why.
s1_b21: The argument was never the homework. This is: in one sentence, your own words — which clue lied, and which rule never did?
s1_b22: And if this felt familiar — it should. Do you remember the two cars at the wall, one that crumpled slowly and one that didn't?
s1_b23: And the figure on the frozen lake, who threw a bag and slid backward — giving nothing away that the ice could keep?
s1_b24: And the steel that bounced, and the clay that just stopped — same momentum, different energy?
s1_b25: This crash was all three at once. That is the concept. Now you know which videos to go back to.
